diagrams.title: Compiler Phases with Target Language Integration
diagrams.description: A block diagram showing the compiler structure highlighting where target language code generation occurs
elements: Target Machine Model dashed box showing instruction set, addressing modes, and register file
diagrams.purpose: Students understand where the target language fits in the compilation process and how the code generator uses target machine characteristics to produce efficient machine code

diagrams.title: Target Machine Instruction Set Model
diagrams.description: Diagram illustrating the components of a target machine instruction set architecture
elements: Instruction Format box with opcode, operand fields
Register Set circle showing general-purpose, special-purpose registers
Addressing Modes hexagon with modes like direct, indirect, indexed, relative
Condition Codes box for status flags
Memory Organization box showing word size, alignment
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the key components of a target machine that the code generator must understand to produce correct and efficient machine instructions

diagrams.title: Addressing Modes Classification Tree
diagrams.description: Hierarchical tree diagram showing all major addressing modes with examples
elements: Register addressing branch with R1 example
Indirect via Register branch with @R1 example
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize all addressing modes, their syntax, and understand which modes are available on different architectures affecting code generation decisions

diagrams.title: Instruction Cost Analysis Model
diagrams.description: Diagram showing factors that contribute to instruction execution cost in terms of clock cycles
elements: Cost Components boxes: Fetch cost, Decode cost, Execute cost, Memory access cost
Memory operand operation higher cost circle
Complex operations (multiplication, division) highest cost circle
Pipeline effect box showing throughput vs latency
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how different instructions have different costs, enabling them to make optimal code generation choices for efficiency

charts.title: RISC vs CISC Target Architecture Comparison
charts.content: Comparison table showing RISC (Reduced Instruction Set) characteristics: fixed-length instructions, load-store architecture, many registers, simple addressing modes vs CISC (Complex Instruction Set): variable-length instructions, memory-to-register operations, few registers, complex addressing modes. Includes examples: RISC-V, MIPS for RISC; x86, VAX for CISC.

charts.title: Addressing Modes Cost Summary
charts.content: Table listing addressing modes with execution cost in clock cycles: Immediate (1 cycle), Register (1 cycle), Direct (2-3 cycles), Indirect (3-4 cycles), Indexed (3-5 cycles), Relative (2-3 cycles). Shows trade-offs between code size and execution speed.

charts.title: Code Generation Decision Process
charts.content: Flowchart showing the code generator decision flow: Start → Analyze operation type → Choose optimal addressing mode → Check register availability → Consider instruction cost → Select best instruction sequence → Emit machine code → End. Shows how target language knowledge guides code generation choices.

charts.title: Target Language Types Classification
charts.content: Classification chart showing: High-level target languages (C, Java bytecode), Assembly language (symbolic instructions), Machine language (binary/hex), Intermediate representations (three-address code, RTL). Each type has different abstraction level and implications for code generator design.

branches: Definition: Output language of code generator
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview connecting all aspects of target language that compiler students must understand for effective code generation and optimization
